concepts.title: Archimedes Principle
concepts.conceptBody:
Archimedes Principle

According to Archimedes' principle,

"When a body is fully or partially submerged in a fluid, a buoyant force or upthrust from the surrounding fluid acts on the body. The force is directed upward and has magnitude equal to the weight mg of the fluid that has been displaced by the body"

The buoyant force on a body in a liquid (fluid) has the magnitude

$\text{F}=\text{mg}={{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}\text{g}$

where $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}=$ the mass of the liquid displaced by the body

${{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}=$ immersed volume of solid

${{\rho }_{\text{L}}}=$ density of liquid

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }g=$ acceleration due to gravity

concepts.title: Law of Floatation
concepts.conceptBody:
Law of Floatation 

Let a body of volume $\text{V}$ and density ${{\rho }_{\text{S}}}$ is floating in a liquid of density ${{\rho }_{\text{L}}}$. Suppose ${{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}$ be the volume of body immersed in the liquid,

For equilibrium of body

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Weight }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Upthrust }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

${{\text{V}}_{{{\rho }_{\text{S}}}}}\text{g}$ $={{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}\text{g}$

$\therefore $ $\frac{{{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}}{\text{V}}$ $~=\frac{{{\rho }_{\text{S}}}}{{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}}$ … (i)

This is the fraction of volume immersed in liquid.

Percentage of volume immersed in liquid

$=\frac{{{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}}{\text{V}}\times 100=\frac{{{\rho }_{\text{S}}}}{{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}}\times 100$

Three possibilities may now arise.

  1. If ${{\rho }_{\text{S}}}<{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}$, only fraction of body will be immersed in the liquid.
  2. If ${{\rho }_{S}}={{\rho }_{L}}$, the whole of the rigid body will be immersed in the liquid.
  3. If ${{\rho }_{S}}>{{\rho }_{L}}$, the body will sink.

concepts.title: Buoyant Force
concepts.conceptBody:
Buoyant Force

In liquid, the apparent weight of the body decreases, and this decrease in its weight is equal to the upthrust acting on the body.

Hence, apparent weight

${{\text{w}}_{\text{app}}}={{\text{V}}_{\text{g}}}\left( {{\rho }_{\text{S}}}-{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}, \right)$

If object is immersed in water, then

$\text{RD}=\frac{\text{ wt}\text{. }\text{ of }\text{ body }\text{ in }\text{ air }}{\text{ Loss }\text{ in }\text{ wt}\text{. }\text{ in }\text{ water }}$

$=\frac{\text{ wt}\text{. }\text{ of }\text{ body }\text{ in }\text{ air }}{\text{ wt}\text{. }\text{ in }\text{ air }-\text{ wt}\text{. }\text{ in }\text{ water }}$

So, by weighing a body in air and in water, we can determine the relative density of the body.

Buoyant Force in Accelerating Fluids 

Suppose a body is dipped inside a liquid of density ${{\rho }_{\text{L}}}$ placed in an elevator moving with an acceleration $\vec{a}$. The buoyant force $\text{F}$ in this case becomes,

$\text{F}=\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{eff }}}$

Here, ${{g}_{\text{eff }}}=\left| \vec{g}-\vec{a} \right|$

For example, if the lift is moving upwards with an acceleration a, the value of ${{g}_{\text{eff }}}$ is $g+a$ and if it is moving downwards with acceleration $\text{a}$, the ${{\text{g}}_{\text{eff }}}$ if $\text{g}-\text{a}$. In a freely falling lift ${{\text{g}}_{\text{eff }}}$ is zero ( as $\text{a}=\text{g}$) and hence, net buoyant force is zero.This is-why, in a freely falling vessel filled with some liquid, the air bubbles do not rise up (which otherwise move up due to buoyant force). The above result can be derived as follows.

Suppose a body is dipped inside a liquid of density ${{\rho }_{\text{L}}}$ in an elevator moving up with an acceleration a. As was done earlier also, replace the body into the liquid by the same liquid of equal volume. The replaced liquid is at rest with respect to the elevator. Thus, this replaced liquid is also moving up with an acceleration a together with the rest of the liquid.

The forces a acting on the replaced liquid are,

(i) the buoyant force $\text{F}$ and

(ii) the weight $\text{mg}$ of the substituted liquid.

From Newton's second law,

$\text{F}-\text{mg}=\text{ma }\text{ or }\text{ F}=\text{m}\left( \text{g}+\text{a} \right)$

Here, $\text{ m}=\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}$

$\therefore \text{ F}=\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}\left( \text{g}+\text{a} \right)=\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{eff }}}$

where ${{g}_{\text{eff }}}=\text{g}+\text{a}$

Example

A certain block weighs 15 N in air. It weighs 12 N when immersed in water. When immersed in another liquid, it weighs 13 N ? Calculate the relative density of (a) the block (b) the other liquid. 

Solution: 

By definition, $RD=\rho /{{\sigma }_{\text{water }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$

(a) So for body $\text{RD}=\frac{{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}}{{{\sigma }_{\text{W}}}}=\frac{\text{ Weight }\text{ of }\text{ body }}{\text{ Weight }\text{ of }\text{ equal }\text{ vol}\text{. }\text{ of }\text{ water }}$

i.e., $\text{RD}=\frac{\text{ Weight }\text{ of }\text{ body }}{\text{ Thrust }\text{ from }\text{ water }}=\frac{{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}}{{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{W}}_{\text{W}}}}$

$=\frac{15}{15-12}=5$

(b) As $\text{Th}=\text{ Loss }\text{ in }\text{ weight }\text{ of }\text{ body }=\text{V}\sigma \text{g}$

So for liquid, $\text{RD}=\frac{{{\rho }_{\text{L}}}}{{{\sigma }_{\text{W}}}}=\frac{\text{T}{{\text{h}}_{\text{L}}}}{\text{T}{{\text{h}}_{\text{W}}}}=\frac{15-13}{15-12}=\frac{2}{3}$

concepts.title: Translatory Equilibrium
concepts.conceptBody:
Translatory-Equilibrium 

When a body of density ${{\rho }_{\text{B}}}$ and volume $\text{V}$ is immersed in a liquid of density $\sigma $, the forces acting on the body are:

(1) The weight of body $\text{W}=\text{mg}=\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}\text{g}$ acting vertically downwards through the centre of gravity of the body.

(2) The upthrust Th $=$ V $\sigma $ g acting vertically upwards through the centre of gravity of the displaced liquid, i.e., centre of buoyancy.

So the following three situations are possible:

(a) The density of body is greater than that of liquid (i.e., ${{\rho }_{\text{B}}}>\sigma $ ). In this situation as weight will be more than upthrust the body will sink. [Fig. (A)]

(b) The density of body is equal to the density of liquid (i.e., ${{\rho }_{\text{B}}}=\sigma $ ). In this situation $\text{W}$ $=$ Th, so the body will float fully submerged in neutral equilibrium anywhere in the liquid. [Fig. (B)]

(c) The density of body is lesser than that of liquid (i.e., ${{\rho }_{\text{B}}}>\sigma $ ). In this situation $\text{W}<\text{Th}$, so the body will move upwards and in equilibrium will float partially immersed in the liquid such that

$\text{W}={{\text{V}}_{\text{in }}}\sigma \text{g}$

[${{\text{V}}_{\text{in }}}$ being the vol. of body in the liquid]

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ or } & \left. \text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}\text{g}={{\text{V}}_{\text{in }}}\sigma \text{g }[\text{as }\text{ W}=\text{mg}={{\rho }_{\text{B}}}\text{Vg} \right] \\ \text{ or } & \text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}={{\text{V}}_{\text{in}}}\sigma \\ \end{array}$

From this all it is clear that:

(1) A body will float in a liquid only and only if ${{\rho }_{\text{B}}}\le \sigma $.

(2) And when a body is floating weight of body is equal to the upthrust, i.e., $\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}\text{g}={{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ бg.

(3) In case of floating as $\text{W}=\text{Th}$, the apparent weight of the floating body will be zero, i.e.,

${{\text{W}}_{\text{app }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\text{W}-\text{Th}=0$

(4) In case of floating as $\text{W}=$ Th implies $\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}\text{g}=\text{V}$ in $\sigma \text{g}$, the equilibrium of floating bodies is unaffected by variation in $\text{g}$ though both thrust and weight depend on ' $\text{g}$ '.

concepts.title: Rotatory Equilibrium
concepts.conceptBody:
Rotatory-Equilibrium 

When a floating body is slightly tilted from equilibrium position, the centre of buoyancy B shifts. The vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy B' and initial vertical line meet at a point $M$ called meta-centre. If the meta-centre $M$ is above the centre of gravity the couple due to forces at G (weight of body W) and at B' (upthrust) tends to bring the body back to its original position [Fig.(B)]. So for rotational equilibrium of floating body the meta-centre must always be higher than the centre of gravity of the body.

However, if meta-centre goes below CG, the couple due to forces at G and B' tends to topple the floating body. [Fig. (C)]

This is why a wooden log cannot be made to float vertical in water or a boat is likely to capsize if the sitting passengers stand on it. In these situation CG becomes higher than MC and so the body will topple if slightly tilted.

concepts.title: Application of Floatation
concepts.conceptBody:
Application of Floatation

(1) Fraction of volume outside the liquid : As in case of a floating body $\text{W}=\text{Th}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}\text{g}={{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\sigma \text{g }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\left( {{\rho }_{\text{B}}}/\sigma \right)\text{V} \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ So }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{\text{out }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\text{V}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\text{V}\left[ 1-\frac{{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}}{\sigma } \right] \\ \end{array}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}_{\text{out }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{{{\text{V}}_{\text{out }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{\text{V}}=\text{V}\left[ 1-\frac{{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}}{\sigma } \right]$

(2) Comparison of densities of floating bodies : As for floating $V{{\rho }_{B}}=V$ in $\sigma $, i.e.,

${{\rho }_{\text{B}}}=\frac{{{\text{V}}_{\text{in}}}}{\text{V}}\sigma ={{\text{f}}_{\text{in}}}\sigma $

So if different bodies are floating in the same liquid,

$\frac{{{\rho }_{\text{B}1}}}{{{\rho }_{\text{B}2}}}=\frac{{{\left( {{\text{f}}_{\text{in}}} \right)}_{1}}}{{{\left( {{\text{f}}_{\text{in}}} \right)}_{2}}}$

(3) Comparison of densities of liquids: If the same body is made to float in different liquids, then

$\text{V}{{\rho }_{\text{B}}}={{\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)}_{1}}{{\sigma }_{1}}={{\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)}_{2}}{{\sigma }_{2}}$

Or $\frac{{{\sigma }_{1}}}{{{\sigma }_{2}}}=\frac{{{\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)}_{2}}}{{{\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)}_{1}}}$

i.e., density of liquid is inversely proportional to the volume of body inside it.

(4) Weighing a body : If a platform of mass $M$ and cross section $A$ is floating in a liquid of density $\sigma $ with its height $\text{h}$ inside the liquid.

$\text{Mg}=\text{hA}\sigma \text{g}$ … (1)

Now if a body of mass $\text{m}$ is placed on it and the platform sinks by $\text{y}$ then

$\left( \text{M}+\text{m} \right)\text{g}=\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)\text{A}\sigma \text{g}$ … (2)

Subtracting Eqn. (1) from (2),

$\text{mg}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Ayg, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ W}\propto \text{y}$ … (3)

So we can determine the weight of a body by placing it on a floating platform and noting the depression of the platform in the liquid by it.

Example

A rod of length 6 m has a mass 12 kg. It is hinged at one end at a distance of 3 m below water surface, (a) What weight must be attached to the other end of the rod so that 5 m of the rod are submerged? (b) Find the magnitude and direction of the force exerted by the hinge on the rod. (Specific gravity of rod is 0.5). 

Solution: 

As shown the forces acting on the rod are :

(1) The weight of $\text{rod}12\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}$ acting downwards through the $\text{CG}$ of the rod, i.e., at a distance of $3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}$ from the hinge.

(2) Force of buoyancy through the CG of displaced liquid vertically upwards. As

$\text{RD}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Weight }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ displaced }\!\!~\!\!\text{ water }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Force }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ buoyancy }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}$

Force of buoyancy $=\left( \frac{5}{6} \right)\times \frac{12\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}}{0.5}=20\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}$

and acts at a distance $2.5\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}$ from the hinge.

(3) Extra weight $\text{w}$ at the other end of the rod at a distance $6\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}$ from $\text{O}$ acting vertically downwards.

(4) Reaction $\text{R}$ at the hinge at $\text{O}$ will be vertical (as here all the forces are vertical, so for horizontal equilibrium of the $\text{rod}{{\text{R}}_{\text{H}}}=0$ ).

So for translatory equilibrium of rod,$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{R}+\text{Th}-\text{W}-\text{w}=0 \\ {} & {} \\ \end{array}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $\text{w}-\text{R}=20\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}-12\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}=8\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}$ … (1)

And for rotational equilibrium of rod (taking moments about $\text{O}$)

$-12\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\times \frac{6}{2}\text{sin}\theta +20\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\times \frac{5}{2}\text{sin}\theta -\text{w}6\text{sin}\theta =0$

Or $\text{w}=\left( 14/6 \right)\text{g}=2.33\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}=2.33\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ kg}$ … (2)

Substituting the value of $\text{w}$ from eq.2 in (1) and solving for $\text{R}$, we get $\text{R}=\left( 2.33-8 \right)\text{gN}=-5.67\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ kg}$

Negative sign implies $R$ is vertically downwards.

examples.title: The fraction of a floating object of volume $V_0$ and density $d_0$ above the surface of a liquid of density $d$ will be

If outer diameter and the density of the bowl are $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{\mathrm{3}}$ respectively, then the inner diameter of the bowl will be

A candle of diameter $d$ is floating on a liquid in a cylindrical container of diameter $D(D \gt d)$ as shown in figure. If it is burning at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~cm} /$ hour then the top of the candle will
